mnemonics.title: Hospital Emergency Room
mnemonics.content: A priority queue works like a hospital ER. Patients are not treated in FIFO order but based on severity (priority). A heart attack patient is treated before a sprained ankle, even if the ankle patient arrived first.

mnemonics.title: U-S Complexity Trade-off
mnemonics.content: Unsorted: insert is Simple (O(1)), delete is Slow (O(n)). Sorted: insert is Slow (O(n)), delete is Simple (O(1)). U-S: the trade-off is always between fast insert and fast delete.

mnemonics.title: A-D Queue Types
mnemonics.content: Ascending = minimum first (A comes first in alphabet, smallest first). Descending = maximum first (D for Down from the top, largest first).

mnemonics.title: Heap Solves Trade-off
mnemonics.content: Both array and linked list implementations have one O(1) and one O(n) operation. Binary heap achieves O(log n) for both insert and delete — the upgrade you'll learn next.

mnemonics.title: Min-PQ Trio: D-P-H
mnemonics.content: Dijkstra's, Prim's, and Huffman coding all use Min-PQ (ascending). Why? Dijkstra finds shortest path (smallest distance), Prim picks smallest edge weight, Huffman combines smallest frequencies.

mnemonics.title: Max-PQ: CPU-ER
mnemonics.content: Max-PQ (descending) is used where higher = more important: CPU scheduling (high priority job runs first) and Emergency room (critical patient first). Remember: CPU and ER both serve the important ones.

mnemonics.title: Sorted Array Trick
mnemonics.content: For a Min-Priority Queue using sorted array: store in DESCENDING priority order (largest at index 0, smallest at index size-1). This makes deleteMin = O(1) — just remove from the end. Opposite for Max-PQ.
